data.title: Melania Trump calls for 'unity' in Minnesota
data.description:
First lady Melania Trump called for unity in Minnesota on Tuesday amid growing outrage over the presence of federal agents in the city following the fatal shooting of ICU nurse Alex Pretti over the weekend.

“I’m calling for unity,” she told “Fox and Friends” in an interview. “I know my husband, the president, had a great call with the governor and the mayor. And they’re working together to make it peaceful.”

The first lady went on to urge demonstrators, who have called out the Trump administration over the surge of immigration enforcement officers to Minneapolis, to avoid violent protests going forward.

“I’m against the riots, so please if you protest, protest in peace,” she said.

The comments come as the administration has notably shifted its messaging surrounding the presence of Border Patrol and Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) in Minnesota following Pretti’s death. The shooting came just over two weeks after an ICE officer shot and killed 37-year-old Renee Good in Minneapolis.

Multiple angles of video captured by witnesses of Pretti’s confrontation with federal law enforcement show an officer shooting him in the back after an altercation in which the protester, filming the officers, sought to help a woman being pepper-sprayed before he was also pepper-sprayed. Video appears to show an officer taking Pretti’s gun and walking away before he was shot.

Following the incident, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said Pretti “attacked” federal law enforcement while “brandishing” a firearm and saying the incident was an example of “domestic terrorism.” Meanwhile, White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller referred to Pretti as a “would-be assassin” who “tried to murder federal law enforcement.”

However, when asked whether Trump agrees with Noem and Miller’s sentiments on the circumstances, White House press secretary Leavitt said she has not heard the president “characterize Mr. Pretti in that way.”

Trump also said Monday that he had productive and “very good” calls with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) on how the two sides can move forward.

Some agents and Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino are expected to leave the city on Tuesday.

data.title: Some Border Patrol agents leaving Minneapolis 'great first step': Council member
data.description:
Some Border Patrol agents are leaving Minneapolis, Minn., after the deaths of two local residents at the hands of federal immigration enforcement agents, which Council Member LaTrisha Vetaw (D) said is a “great first step” to deescalating tensions. 

Greg Bovino, commander-at-large of the U.S. Border Patrol, is expected to depart Minnesota on Tuesday along with some of his agents, according to multiple reports.

“That that is a great first step. I talked to Mayor Jacob Frey about 20-30 minutes ago, and he told me that he thinks that they’re going to be some agents leaving as early as tomorrow,” Vetaw said during a Monday night appearance on NewsNation’s “The Hill.”

“He told me that he spoke with the president and actually had a really good conversation with the president and is feeling good about how we move forward as a city, as a state, and so he I left the conversation feeling extremely optimistic,” she added.

In a post on X, Frey said President Trump told him the “present situation” in Minneapolis “can’t continue,” echoing Republican and Democratic lawmakers alike. Residents have gathered in large numbers to protest the killing of Alex Pretti, who was shot several times while helping a woman who was pushed to the ground by a group of agents.

Bystander footage shows Pretti, an ICU nurse, assisting her before being wrestled to the ground and shot. Bovino said the shooting was justified because Pretti was carrying a gun, but Trump administration officials have said the agents’ actions are under internal review. Footage of the incident appears to show a gun being taken away from the scrum before Pretti is shot.

The president said White House border czar Tom Homan would be sent to Minneapolis to oversee local operations and report to him directly after some videos showing federal agents tackling protesters spread on social media.

Vetaw said she hopes to see things cool down after Frey’s conversation with Trump.

“I think the temperature is being toned down between our leaders, and that feels really good, right?” Vetaw told anchor Blake Burman.

“As someone who is on the ground every day and talking to folks, it’s going to feel good to be able to answer my phone tonight and say that our mayor and our governor spoke with the president, and they’re talking about how we get some relief in this state,” she added.

data.title: A faster way to power AI ambitions: Make your own electricity
data.description:
The Trump administration and a bipartisan group of 13 governors announced an agreement urging the PJM Interconnection — the largest electric grid operator in the U.S. — to “…expand the energy supply, safeguard affordability and support rapid expansion of data-center development.” These are commendable goals, but the unfortunate reality is that the shared grid is ill-equipped to power data centers quickly or fairly.

The U.S. electricity sector is a slow-moving maze of regulations, shaped by decade-long transmission approvals, time-intensive interconnection studies for new generators and large new customers, and overlapping layers of state, regional and federal bureaucracy. Neither the 13 governors nor the president can easily order PJM to do anything, nor is more government intervention a recipe for long-term success.

The regulatory thicket surrounding the electricity industry was tolerable when the pace of change was slow. However, with the rise of AI and renewed growth from manufacturing and electrification, we can no longer endure a sclerotic grid. 

Here’s the rub: Reforming the entire industry would be messy and slow. Unwinding electricity’s regulatory complexity without adding risk and political uncertainty is a Herculean task that cannot happen quickly enough to relieve today’s crunch. Further, deep reform gambles the fate of the entire country — a common quip is that electricity is just 5 percent of the economy, but it powers the other 95 percent. We must keep the grid reliable and affordable for everyday Americans. 

But there is a way to enable the electricity buildout required by modern industry while protecting consumers and guarding against blackouts. We can allow entrepreneurs and private investors to work outside of the existing sector to establish new, private utilities and new grids free from the regulations that dominate the shared grid.

Sen. Tom Cotton’s (R-Ark.) DATA Act of 2026 offers a consequential fix. It would exempt certain new electricity providers from the federal regulations that apply to the broader grid. The bill would clear a path for new, off-grid providers to move quickly to serve AI and data center demand.

By focusing on AI and data centers, as well as other large industrial loads, these new utilities and grids would serve large and sophisticated businesses that buy products and services from other parties all the time without regulatory oversight. For these customers, buying electricity should be no different. And with no physical connection to regulated utilities, these new utilities could serve new customers without impacting the cost or reliability of the existing grid.

As it stands, federal utility regulations can easily add years to project timelines. Many of these rules are necessary to maintain reliability, since electricity must be balanced in real time, 24 hours a day, seven days a week. Policymakers are reluctant to tinker with the entire grid because the downside is too great — blackouts are hazardous to public health and politically toxic. 

Even well-meaning regulations create uncertainty for investors: An investor may think they have a great project, but it can be shot down by regulators or delayed to the point of economic extinction. Worse, the rules of the road can change every four years.

The DATA Act would promote speed and certainty. It doesn’t use taxpayer money or risk burdening existing ratepayers, who have little choice but to buy their electricity from the local monopoly. If entrepreneurs and private investors are willing to risk their money and create an off-grid utility, the DATA Act would effectively say, “good luck and Godspeed!” They would sink or swim based on their ability to meet customers’ needs. The cost of failure — or a potential AI bubble — would hit investors rather than ordinary ratepayers.

The biggest benefit of the DATA Act may prove to be what the electricity sector has lacked for about a century: innovation. For too long, innovation in the sector has moved at a snail’s pace. As our grids became “too big to fail,” reliability — not speed or efficiency — became the objective of the country’s regulatory framework. Unfortunately, innovation can put reliability at risk.  

Entrepreneurs and private investors, on the other hand, embrace risk. Building a new off-grid utility is inherently risky. It will need to be innovative in the hope of outcompeting existing utilities for new business. And without federal regulators poring over the new utility’s proposed innovations, it would be free to try new things. The good and bad ideas alike will be instructive to entrepreneurs and regulators, and the best ideas can be safely imported into the regulated utility space.

Letting AI data centers and other large customers use new, off-grid power providers is not a bet against regulation, regulators, or the regulated grid. It is simply an acknowledgement that a regulatory system built for a 20th century monopoly grid cannot power a 21st century AI economy. Giving entrepreneurs the room to build around that system and create something new and dynamic is the fastest and safest way forward.

Travis Fisher is director of energy and environmental policy studies at the Cato Institute. Glen Lyons is founder and chief advocate of Advocates for Consumer Regulated Electricity.

data.title: Chris Madel: 'This is not the United States … that we want to be'
data.description:
Chris Madel, the attorney who ended his GOP campaign for Minnesota governor Monday after the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by a Border Patrol official, slammed the Trump administration’s immigration enforcement operations.

“There are people right now that are living in fear, that are United States citizens, that are staying home right now and feeling like they can’t go outside without bringing their papers,” Madel told NewsNation’s Chris Cuomo in an interview. “They’re bringing their passports out.”

“This is not the United States that any of us grew up and believe, in our hearts, that we want to be,” he added.

Earlier in the day, Madel dropped his gubernatorial bid, saying in a video that he “cannot support the national Republican stated retribution on the citizens of our state, nor can I count myself a member of a party that would do so.”

Madel, a lawyer representing Jonathan Ross — the Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer who fatally shot 37-year-old Renee Good in Minneapolis earlier this month — noted that while he supports deporting violent undocumented migrants, the administration’s Minnesota operation “has expanded far beyond its stated focus on true public safety threats.”

“United States citizens, particularly those of color, live in fear,” the attorney continued. “United States citizens are carrying papers to prove their citizenship. That’s wrong.”

Pretti’s death, which came less than three weeks after Ross shot and killed Good in her car, sparked further backlash against the federal presence in the North Star State. The administration surged ICE and Border Patrol personnel to the state earlier this month amid a federal probe into social services fraud.

President Trump spoke with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) on Monday. Frey wrote on social platform X that the president “agreed the present situation can’t continue” in the city and that some federal agents will begin leaving the area on Tuesday, including Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino.

Frey also noted he will meet Tuesday with White House border czar Tom Homan, who Trump announced on Monday would take over operations in Minnesota.

data.title: The Supreme Court could bring Texas-style gerrymandering to your state
data.description:
Texas voters have had a rough year. They’ve endured a nasty statewide redistricting fight that disenfranchises voters of color under the pretense of partisanship. They’ve watched that map first get blocked by a Trump-appointed district judge, then put into effect again by the Supreme Court.

As a result, Texas voters face unprecedented confusion. One district in the Houston area faces the prospect of holding four elections for the same seat in a single year. Now, the Supreme Court might force everyone in the country to endure such confusion and disenfranchisement. 

In the coming months, the Supreme Court is likely to issue a ruling in Louisiana v. Callais, gutting Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act –– one of the only remaining federal protections against racially discriminatory redistricting. This would allow the chaos of Texas’s redistricting fight to reverberate across the country even more than it already has.

The Supreme Court has been weakening legal protections against discriminatory redistricting for more than a decade. In 2013, the court gutted the Voting Rights Act’s preclearance process, which had been one of our most effective tools to block discriminatory maps. Then the court decided that the federal courts couldn’t intervene when lawmakers rig maps for partisan reasons.

Last year, the Supreme Court added the extraordinary requirement that challengers to discriminatory maps can’t win unless they provide their own non-discriminatory map that produces the exact same partisan outcome — making it near-impossible to challenge racial discrimination in places like the south, where race and party can be closely related.

This tortured legal landscape makes Section 2 critical. Section 2 was enacted to ensure that political processes were equally open to all voters. Under Section 2, courts are supposed to look at the facts to decide whether a state’s map draws people of color out of political power — exactly the situation that gave rise to Callais and the Fifth Circuit decision requiring Louisiana to draw a new, fairer map with two majority-Black districts.

But despite its own precedent, the court seems poised to make a sweeping ruling that states cannot consider race at all in redistricting, even when that consideration is necessary to prevent discriminatory outcomes. If the court guts that key element of Section 2, it will allow states, counties, cities and school boards to boldly draw racist maps with very limited legal remedy. 

The consequences are dire. If Callais is decided unfavorably, all levels of government will rush to redraw their maps in discriminatory ways, disrupting the 2026 primary elections. 

In other words, the Supreme Court will invite states to follow the same playbook that it allowed in Texas’s redistricting battle. That battle began in July, when the Department of Justice sent a letter urging Texas to dismantle several majority-minority congressional districts based on the inaccurate argument that Section 2 no longer protected those districts. Two days later, Gov. Greg Abbott (R) called a special session of the Texas legislature asking them to redistrict in light of the Justice Department’s racial — not partisan — concerns.

The Texas legislature quickly pushed through a map dismantling the Justice Department’s targeted districts, with no opportunity for public testimony. The new map allowed white Texans — who comprise only 40 percent of the population — to control 79 percent of the state’s congressional seats. This move was astounding, especially since 95 percent of Texas’s population growth since 2010 has been in communities of color.

At first, it seemed like the courts would stop this charade. A Trump-appointed federal district judge concluded that race, not partisanship, predominated in the Texas redistricting. Despite this finding, the Supreme Court allowed Texas’s gerrymander rush-job to stand. The court justified this decision in part by saying that it couldn’t intervene “on the eve of an election,” even though the election in question is 11 months away. Now, Texans are stuck with a racist map for the 2026 primary.

But if the Supreme Court’s logic is that a 2025 decision pausing Texas’s map would throw 2026 elections into chaos, then a Callais decision destroying Section 2 would be hypocritical at best and nakedly cynical at worst. It would confirm that the court is willing to throw out its precedents to undermine our democracy. That reasoning would grant states across the country carte blanche to unfairly redistrict in an election year, with courts forbidden to step in.

The Supreme Court likes to pretend that it is immune from public accountability, but it should not be allowed to gut Section 2 without a whimper. We must highlight the hypocrisy and inconsistency of this court’s rulings. We must pressure Congress to reform national redistricting law. And most importantly, we must prepare for power grabs at every level of government aimed at dismantling hard-won progress for Black, Latino, Asian and Native American representation across the country. 

Without Section 2, your future may look a lot like Texas’s very recent past. 

Veronikah Warms is the Voting Rights Policy attorney for the Texas Civil Rights Project and has worked in the Texas legislative sphere pre- and post-licensure for the last decade.

data.title: Fetterman calls on Trump to fire Noem: 'Americans have died'
data.description:
Centrist Democratic Sen. John Fetterman (Pa.) called on President Trump Tuesday to fire Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem after the fatal shooting of a second protester in Minneapolis over the weekend.

“President Trump: I make a direct appeal to immediately fire Secretary Noem. Americans have died. She is betraying DHS’s core mission and trashing your border security legacy,” Fetterman said in a statement.

He warned that Trump is at risk of repeating the mistake of former President Biden, who stood by his own embattled Homeland Security secretary, Alejandro Mayorkas, despite many complaints from Republicans over his handling of the job.

“DO NOT make the mistake President Biden made for not firing a grossly incompetent DHS Secretary,” the Pennsylvania Democrat said.

Fetterman on Monday called for federal officials to halt the immigration enforcement operation in Minneapolis known as Operation Metro Surge but he says he won’t risk a partial government shutdown at the end of the week by voting to block funding for the Department of Homeland Security.

“Ms. Good and Mr. Pretti should still be alive. My family grieves for theirs,” the senator said, referring to the two protesters who were shot and killed by federal officers.

“The operation in Minneapolis should stand down and immediately end. It has become an ungovernable and dangerous urban theatre for civilians and law enforcement that is incompatible with the American spirit,” he added.

His comments come as other Democrats have called for Noem’s impeachment or resignation after she called Alex Pretti, the ICU nurse shot and killed by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis Saturday, a “domestic terrorist,” despite a lack of evidence to support the claim.

Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) is one Democrat calling for the DHS chief to be impeached.

 “She lied about a citizen to justify his murder. And that is not leadership. The president should ask for her resignation. But if not, I do think the House should impeach,” Gallego said in an interview with MS NOW.

Trump and White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt have distanced themselves from Noem’s rhetoric.

Asked Monday if the president agreed with Noem’s characterization of the shooting incident, Leavitt told reporters: “I have not heard the president characterize Mr. Pretti in that way.”

Noem is scheduled to testify before the Senate Judiciary Committee on March. 3.

Sen. Pete Welch (D-Vt.), a member of the Judiciary Committee, on Monday called Noem “a real problem.”

“Noem’s got to go. I mean, we have the head of this department who spends a lot of her time in self-promotion, the $200 million video shoot in Mount Rushmore,” Welch said in an MS NOW interview. “And then when there is a serious, serious situation involving the death of two [Minnesota] citizens, she calls them domestic terrorists.

data.title: Republicans appeal ruling throwing out only GOP-controlled NYC House seat
data.description:
Republicans are appealing a ruling that throws out Rep. Nicole Malliotakis’s (R) seat in New York and orders the creation of a new House map ahead of November.

Attorneys representing several members of the New York State Board of Elections, in addition to Malliotakis and a group of voters, appealed the ruling on Monday. Republicans are appealing the decision in both the New York State Court of Appeals and with the appellate division of the New York State Supreme Court.

Attorneys for Malliotakis and the group of voters told the Court of Appeals in a letter on Monday that Republicans also planned to file motions with both courts requesting a pause on the New York State Supreme Court’s decision to institute a new map in the state.

“Time is of the essence in this matter, as the Order has now thrown New York’s 2026 Congressional Election — slated to begin on February 24, 2026 — into chaos,” Bennet Moskowitz, the attorney for the House Republican and group of voters, wrote.

Moskowitz asked the courts to make a decision by Feb. 10 on whether the courts would pause the ruling and allow the current New York House map to be used for the 2026 midterms. If they didn’t rule by then, Moskowitz said they would take it up immediately to the U.S. Supreme Court.

Acting New York state Supreme Court Justice Jeffrey Pearlman, an appointee of Gov. Kathy Hochul (D), ruled earlier this month that New York’s 11th Congressional District, which includes Staten Island and some of southern Brooklyn, was unconstitutionally configured. He ordered for the congressional lines to be tossed out and a new House map made to be used in time for the 2026 midterms.

Malliotakis’s district went for Trump by over 24 points in 2024, and a new map could offer Democrats another pickup opportunity ahead of the November elections.

The court decision has already created uncertainty for nearby House districts, which are likely to see their congressional lines redrawn should a new map be implemented.

The decision could impact candidate filing deadlines and the timing of the state’s congressional primaries — an issue that the Empire State contended with once before during the 2022 midterms after a judge struck down a Democratic-favored map and ordered a court-appointed special master to create one instead.

data.title: Natalie Portman: Trump, Noem, ICE actions 'outrageous'
data.description:
Natalie Portman is calling out President Trump and Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem following the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by a federal law enforcement agent in Minnesota, saying what’s happening in the United States amid the administration’s deportation efforts is “obscene.”

“I’m getting emotional because it’s really been — it’s such a horrible day. It’s been a horrible week,” the “Black Swan” star, appearing to tear up, said during a Saturday interview ahead of the premiere of “The Gallerist” at the Sundance Film Festival in Utah.

“It’s very joyful to be at a film festival and in a creative community celebrating film together, but what is happening in our country is just obscene,” Portman said.

“What Trump, and Kristi Noem and ICE [Immigration and Customs Enforcement] are doing to our citizens, and undocumented people, is outrageous and needs to end,” the Academy Award winner said.

The 44-year-old actor’s comments followed the shooting death earlier in the day of Pretti, a 37-year-old ICU nurse, by a Customs and Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis. Pretti’s death came weeks after an ICE officer shot and killed a 37-year-old woman, Renee Good, during the Department of Homeland Security’s immigration crackdown in Minnesota. 

Trump said Sunday that his administration was “reviewing everything and will come out with a determination” regarding Pretti’s death.

“I don’t like any shooting. I don’t like it,” Trump said in an interview with The Wall Street Journal.

Portman told reporters at the red carpet event that she was “very moved” by “all of the people coming out and supporting each other, community, and showing the best of America — really being incredible neighbors and citizens.”

“But it’s just too much,” she said. “It needs to end.”

data.title: Live updates: Senate returns amid DHS funding uproar as shutdown deadline looms
data.description:
The Senate heads back to Washington on Tuesday as a partial government shutdown looms. But Democrats are eyeing funding for Department of Homeland Security (DHS), after the weekend killing of a Minneapolis man by a Border Patrol (CBP) agent.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) has indicated he intends to move forward with a six-bill funding package, which includes DHS, despite Democrats’ threats to block the measure. Thune must confront whether he can rally his conference and the White House around a modified bill that separates DHS funding from the package to avoid another shutdown.

It also comes as President Trump is making adjustments to his signature initiatives in the wake of Alex Pretti’s death on Saturday. Border czar Tom Homan will arrive Tuesday in Minneapolis, replacing Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino.

The shooting ignited a firestorm on both sides of the aisle, with Democrats calling for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to be fired or impeached, and Republican lawmakers expressing grave concern and calling for hearings.

Trump on Tuesday will head to Des Moines, Iowa, to deliver remarks.

Follow live below.

data.title: The facts are in: Trump's return-to-office mandate backfired
data.description:
On Jan. 20, 2025, a signed return to in-person work memo landed on agency desks and sent managers scrambling. At the Social Security Administration, routine telework collapsed within weeks, even as phone lines stayed jammed. 

A fresh watchdog report shows the human cost: The very flexibility that drew talent in now pushes talent out. The report comes from the Government Accountability Office, and it focuses on how the decree rippled through hiring, training and retention. 

Social Security already carries a heavy service load, so every resignation shows up in longer waits and slower decisions for the public. The report’s findings, paired with broader federal research, point to a simple lesson: telework works best when leaders manage it, measure it and use it to keep skills in house. 

Government Accountability Office investigators tracked telework at the Social Security Administration from July 2019 through May 2025 and found a sharp cliff after the White House memo. Telework hours fell from 35 percent of total hours in January through March 2025 to 13 percent in April through May 2025, a drop that matched the new posture. That speed matters, because SSA employees had built their lives and budgets around flexibility.  

Agency leaders told investigators that telework acted as a recruitment lever during a tight labor market. In a fiscal 2023 new-hire survey, more than half of new employees said telework ranked as a very important factor in applying and accepting the job. Managers also described candidates who expected hybrid schedules as a baseline benefit, especially in high traffic metro areas.

Retention signals flashed even before Trump’s decree. About 37 percent of Social Security Administration respondents to the 2024 employee viewpoint survey planned to leave within a year. Among those planning an exit, almost half said telework or remote options in their unit shaped that decision. Frontline staff singled out newer hires and retirement-eligible experts as the most ready to move, since both groups value lighter commutes and focused work time.

The Government Accountability Office warned about skills gaps in mission critical roles just as the Social Security Administration announced a 50,000-employee target for in-person work on Feb. 28, 2025, in an agency workforce plan aimed at cutting costs. 

Federal law already treats telework as a management tool and a continuity asset through the Telework Enhancement Act that requires policies, training and reporting. That framework exists because agencies use telework to keep services running during weather events, security incidents and public health disruptions.

The Office of Personnel Management’s fiscal 2023 telework status report describes agencies that used hybrid schedules as lifting recruitment and retention while sustaining performance. In the same report, Treasury’s Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau credits telework with shrinking its footprint in Washington by 22 percent and saving nearly $1 million in facilities costs, a concrete real estate savings example that private employers envy. EPA reports about $7 million a year in avoided transit subsidy spending thanks to current telework levels — another budget relief that frees up funds for mission work. 

Those dollars connect directly to retention because agencies reinvest savings in better tools, training and workspace design. When leaders pair clear performance standards with predictable flexibility, they win a recruiting edge and keep accountability high. That formula matters more in government than in most sectors because hiring cycles run long, and specialized knowledge takes years to build. 

Long before the pandemic, the Merit Systems Protection Board laid out a business case: with solid supervision, telework delivers benefits while maintaining productivity, as detailed in its telework evidence. The board also highlights continuity of operations, reduced real estate pressure, and better work-life balance from shorter commutes. 

In a 2024 performance evaluation report, the Government Accountability Office urged agencies to measure telework impacts on customer service, training and organizational health. In its new study of Social Security, the watchdog agency repeats that call and presses the agency to update its human capital plan and keep evaluating telework where offices still use it. 

Trump’s administration framed the return push around supervision and fairness, echoing language from the Jan. 22, 2025 guidance memo. But the findings reveal a hidden trade-off: forcing the same schedule on every job drains the very talent that the public relies on for timely benefits decisions. A smarter approach uses job-based eligibility, transparent metrics and targeted onsite time for training, mentoring and complex customer work. Agencies that build that system keep their best people longer, save money and deliver service with steadier staffing. 

Return mandates feel decisive, yet the audit trail shows decisive moves can create staffing turbulence. Employees who joined for flexibility start scanning the market for something better. Managers lose years of expertise with every departure. 

Government has the tools to do better: law, reporting and research tying telework to continuity and cost control. Leaders can honor in-person collaboration by designing office time around work needs, using data to adjust. When the federal workforce stays whole, the public feels it first. 

Gleb Tsipursky, Ph.D., serves as CEO of the hybrid work consultancy Disaster Avoidance Experts and authored the best-seller “Returning to the Office and Leading Hybrid and Remote Teams.”
